preamble.summary: A festival psalm attributed to Asaph that opens with exuberant summons to praise — shout, sing, blow the ram's horn — then pivots sharply as God's own voice breaks in. The divine speech dominates the psalm from verse 6 onward, recounting the exodus deliverance, the testing at Meribah, and the first commandment's demand for exclusive loyalty. God laments Israel's refusal to listen, describes what He did in response (handing them over to their own stubborn hearts), and closes with a devastating conditional: if only My people would listen, I would feed them with the finest wheat and honey from the rock.
preamble.remarkable: The structural pivot at verse 6 is one of the most dramatic in the Psalter. What begins as a congregational hymn suddenly becomes a divine oracle. God speaks in first person — 'I relieved his shoulder of the burden' — and the worshippers become an audience to their own history retold by the one who shaped it. The closing conditional (lu ammi shomea li, 'if only My people would listen to Me') is among the most poignant lines God speaks in the Hebrew Bible. It reveals divine longing — God as the rejected lover who still extends the offer. The psalm's movement from celebration to divine lament mirrors the trajectory of Israel's own story: from deliverance to disobedience.
preamble.friction: The superscription assigns this to Asaph, which places it within the Asaphite collection (Psalms 73-83). The reference to Joseph in verse 6 (edut bi-Yehoseph) is unusual — most psalms use Jacob or Israel. The use of Joseph may signal northern tribal identity or invoke the exodus generation specifically. The phrase 'I tested you at the waters of Meribah' (v. 8) echoes Exodus 17 and Numbers 20, but the Hebrew selah at verse 8 makes the exact division of the divine speech debated among commentators.
preamble.connections: The call to blow the shofar at the new moon and full moon (v. 4) connects to the festival calendar of Leviticus 23. The divine speech recounting exodus deliverance parallels Psalm 95:7-11, which similarly moves from praise to divine warning about hardened hearts. The closing image of honey from the rock (v. 17) echoes Deuteronomy 32:13, where God feeds Israel honey from the crag and oil from flinty rock. The 'I am the LORD your God who brought you up out of Egypt' formula (v. 11) quotes the Decalogue preamble (Exodus 20:2) verbatim.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Gittith, A Psalm of Asaph.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. On the Gittith. Of Asaph.
translator_notes: The term gittit is of uncertain meaning — possibly a musical instrument from Gath, a winepress melody (from gat, 'winepress'), or a tune associated with the grape harvest. Its appearance in Psalms 8, 81, and 84 may link these psalms to autumn festival celebrations. The Asaph attribution places this psalm within the Levitical guild of temple musicians established by David (1 Chronicles 16:4-5).

verses.text_hebrew: הַ֭רְנִינוּ לֵאלֹהִ֣ים עוּזֵּ֑נוּ הָ֝רִ֗יעוּ לֵאלֹהֵ֥י יַעֲקֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Sing aloud unto God our strength: make a joyful noise unto the God of Jacob.
verses.rendering:
Shout for joy to God our strength!
Raise a shout to the God of Jacob!
translator_notes: The verb harninu ('shout, cry out in joy') and hariu ('raise a shout, sound a blast') are both high-volume, communal actions — this is not private meditation but public, full-throated worship. The designation 'God of Jacob' rather than 'God of Israel' may preserve an older tribal designation or emphasize the patriarch's personal story of wrestling and transformation.

verses.text_kjv: Take a psalm, and bring hither the timbrel, the pleasant harp with the psaltery.
verses.rendering:
Lift up a song and strike the tambourine,
the sweet-sounding lyre with the harp!
translator_notes: The verb se'u ('lift up, carry') paired with zimrah ('song, melody') creates the image of physically raising music like an offering. The tof ('tambourine, hand drum'), kinnor ('lyre'), and nevel ('harp') form the standard ensemble of temple worship. The kinnor was David's instrument (1 Samuel 16:23); the nevel was a larger, deeper-toned stringed instrument.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּקְע֣וּ בַחֹ֣דֶשׁ שׁוֹפָ֑ר בַּ֝כֵּ֗סֶה לְי֣וֹם חַגֵּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Blow up the trumpet in the new moon, in the time appointed, on our solemn feast day.
verses.rendering:
Blow the ram's horn at the new moon,
at the full moon, on the day of our feast!
translator_notes: The word keseh (or kese) means 'full moon' or 'the covering/concealment' — most likely the full moon when the moon is fully 'covered' with light. The pairing of chodesh ('new moon') with keseh ('full moon') spans the entire lunar cycle. The feast in view is almost certainly Sukkot (Tabernacles), which begins at the full moon of the seventh month (Tishrei), preceded by the shofar blast of Rosh Hashanah at the new moon.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י חֹ֣ק לְיִשְׂרָאֵ֣ל ה֑וּא מִ֝שְׁפָּ֗ט לֵאלֹהֵ֥י יַעֲקֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: For this was a statute for Israel, and a law of the God of Jacob.
verses.rendering:
For it is a statute for Israel,
a ruling from the God of Jacob.
translator_notes: The word choq ('statute, decree, something inscribed') and mishpat ('ruling, judgment, ordinance') together convey that this festival observance is not optional tradition but divinely mandated law. The God of Jacob did not suggest celebration — He commanded it.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵ֤דוּת ׀ בִּֽיה֘וֹסֵ֤ף שָׂמ֗וֹ בְּ֭צֵאתוֹ עַל־אֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרָ֑יִם שְׂפַ֖ת לֹא־יָדַ֣עְתִּי אֶשְׁמָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: This he ordained in Joseph for a testimony, when he went out through the land of Egypt: where I heard a language that I understood not.
verses.rendering:
He established it as a testimony in Joseph
when he went out against the land of Egypt.
I heard a language I did not know:
translator_notes: The use of Joseph for Israel is rare — it appears also in Psalms 77:16, 78:67, and 80:2. In each case the context involves the exodus or the northern tribes. The shift from 'he' to 'I' in this verse marks the beginning of the divine oracle that dominates the rest of the psalm.

key_terms.semantic_range: testimony, witness, decree, covenant stipulation, evidence
key_terms.note: edut derives from ud ('to testify, to bear witness') and in legal contexts means a binding covenant stipulation. When God establishes an edut in Joseph, He is placing a covenant obligation on the people — the festivals are not mere celebration but covenant testimony, a witness to what God has done.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲסִיר֣וֹתִי מִסֵּ֣בֶל שִׁכְמ֑וֹ כַּ֝פָּ֗יו מִדּ֥וּד תַּעֲבֹֽרְנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: I removed his shoulder from the burden: his hands were delivered from the pots.
verses.rendering:
I relieved his shoulder of the burden;
his hands were freed from the basket.
verses.expanded_rendering: God now speaks directly, recounting the exodus in visceral, physical language. The word sevel ('burden, forced labor') is the technical term for the corvee labor of Egypt — the same word used in Exodus 1:11 and 5:4-5. The image is of a bent back under crushing weight, and God says hasiroti ('I removed it'). The dud ('basket, pot, cauldron') refers to the clay-carrying baskets or brick molds of Egyptian slavery. God freed the hands that were raw from forced labor. The physical specificity is the point: God does not speak in abstractions about deliverance. He speaks about shoulders and hands.
translator_notes: The word dud can mean 'pot, cauldron, or basket.' In the context of Egyptian slavery, it most likely refers to the baskets used to carry clay or bricks (cf. Exodus 1:14, where Israel's labor involves mortar and brick). Some translations render it 'pots' following KJV, but 'basket' better fits the construction imagery of the Egyptian bondage.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּצָּרָ֥ה קָרָ֗אתָ וָאֲחַ֫לְּצֶ֥ךָּ אֶ֭עֶנְךָ בְּסֵ֣תֶר רַ֑עַם אֶבְחָֽנְךָ֨ עַל־מֵ֖י מְרִיבָ֣ה סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou calledst in trouble, and I delivered thee; I answered thee in the secret place of thunder; I proved thee at the waters of Meribah. Selah.
verses.rendering:
In distress you called and I rescued you.
I answered you from the hidden place of thunder.
I tested you at the waters of Meribah. Selah.
translator_notes: Meribah (from riv, 'to contend, to quarrel') is the place where Israel quarreled with God over the lack of water. The name itself means 'Quarreling' or 'Contention.' God's statement that He tested Israel there reverses the perspective of Exodus 17:7, where the people tested God. Both testings happened simultaneously: Israel tested God's patience while God tested Israel's trust.

key_terms.note: Merivah derives from riv ('to contend, to bring a legal case'). It names both the event (Israel's quarrel with God) and the place. The word carries legal overtones — riv is the term for a covenant lawsuit. At Meribah, Israel effectively put God on trial, demanding that He prove Himself. God now says He was the one doing the testing.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁמַ֣ע עַ֭מִּי וְאָעִ֣ידָה בָּ֑ךְ יִ֝שְׂרָאֵ֗ל אִם־תִּשְׁמַֽע־לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear, O my people, and I will testify unto thee: O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me;
verses.rendering:
Listen, My people, and I will warn you.
O Israel, if only you would listen to Me!
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb a'idah ('I will testify, I will warn') is the same root as edut ('testimony') from verse 6 — God is both the one who established the testimony and the one who now testifies. The conditional im tishma li ('if you would listen to Me') introduces the great 'if' that structures the rest of the psalm. Everything that follows hangs on whether Israel will shema — hear, listen, obey. The word shema carries all three meanings simultaneously in Hebrew, and all three are needed here. God is not asking Israel to merely perceive sound; He is asking for obedient attention.
translator_notes: The construction shema...im tishma ('hear...if you would hear') uses the same verb twice, creating an envelope: the command to listen frames the condition of listening. It echoes the Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4, where hearing is the foundational act of covenant fidelity.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹֽא־יִהְיֶ֣ה בְ֭ךָ אֵ֣ל זָ֑ר וְלֹ֥א תִ֝שְׁתַּחֲוֶ֗ה לְאֵ֣ל נֵכָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: There shall no strange god be in thee; neither shalt thou worship any strange god.
verses.rendering:
There must be no foreign god among you.
You must not bow down to an alien god.
translator_notes: The phrase el zar ('foreign god') and el nekhar ('alien god') form a synonymous parallel that echoes the first commandment (Exodus 20:3). The word zar means 'strange, foreign, unauthorized' and nekhar means 'alien, unrecognized, from outside.' Together they describe any deity that is not the LORD — not merely foreign in origin but unauthorized in identity. God is quoting the Decalogue's opening demand back to His people.

verses.text_hebrew: אָנֹכִ֨י ׀ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֶ֗יךָ הַֽ֭מַּעַלְךָ מֵאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרָ֑יִם הַרְחֶב־פִּ֝֗יךָ וַאֲמַלְאֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt: open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it.
verses.rendering:
I am the LORD your God
who brought you up from the land of Egypt.
Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it.
translator_notes: The phrase harchev pikha ('open your mouth wide') uses the Hiphil of rachav ('to be wide, spacious'). It means to open as wide as possible — to hold nothing back in asking, to receive with full capacity. The promise va'amal'ehu ('and I will fill it') is unconditional in its grammar: God does not say 'I might fill it.' The only condition is the opening.

key_terms.rendered_as: I am the LORD your God
key_terms.semantic_range: the self-identification formula of the covenant God
key_terms.note: This is the Decalogue preamble — the identity claim that precedes all commandments. Before God commands anything, He identifies Himself by what He has done: brought Israel out of Egypt. The commandments are not cold law; they flow from a relationship established by rescue. Every demand God makes is grounded in this prior act of deliverance.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹא־שָׁמַ֣ע עַ֭מִּי לְקוֹלִ֑י וְ֝יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל לֹא־אָ֥בָה לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But my people would not hearken to my voice; and Israel would none of me.
verses.rendering:
But My people did not listen to My voice,
and Israel would have none of Me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The devastating conjunction ve-lo ('but not') breaks the psalm open. After the tenderness of 'open your mouth wide and I will fill it,' the response is refusal. Lo shama ammi le-qoli ('My people did not listen to My voice') — the very shema that was commanded in verse 9 is here denied. And the second line intensifies: ve-Yisra'el lo avah li ('and Israel would have none of Me'). The verb avah means 'to be willing, to consent, to desire.' Israel did not merely fail to obey — Israel did not want God. The divine voice here carries unmistakable grief. This is not the fury of a king whose subjects rebelled; it is the sorrow of a parent whose child turned away.
translator_notes: The verb avah ('to be willing, to consent') is a word of volition, not capacity. Israel's refusal was not inability to hear but unwillingness to respond. The phrase lo avah li ('would not consent to Me') personalizes the rejection: it is not God's law that Israel rejected but God Himself.

verses.text_kjv: So I gave them up unto their own hearts' lust: and they walked in their own counsels.
verses.rendering:
So I let them go in the stubbornness of their hearts;
they walked by their own plans.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase va'ashallchehu bi-sherirut libbam ('I let them go in the stubbornness of their heart') reveals the most terrifying form of divine judgment: not punishment but release. God did not strike Israel — He released them. The word sherirut ('stubbornness, firmness, hardness') describes a heart that has calcified around its own preferences. And mo'atsotehem ('their own counsels, plans, schemes') are the substitute for God's guidance. When God lets go, the human heart becomes its own god and its own advisor. This is the judgment Paul describes in Romans 1:24 ('God gave them over') — the withdrawal of restraint, the permission to have what you insist on wanting.
translator_notes: The verb shillach ('to send away, to let go, to release') in the Piel is the same verb used for Pharaoh releasing Israel from Egypt. The irony is pointed: God once sent Israel out of bondage; now He sends them into the bondage of their own desires. Freedom from God becomes the worst captivity.

key_terms.semantic_range: stubbornness, hardness, firmness, obstinacy of heart/mind
key_terms.note: sherirut appears almost exclusively in Jeremiah (16 times) and here. It describes a heart that has hardened into its own shape and will not be reformed. The 'heart' (lev) in Hebrew is the seat of will and decision, not emotion. A stubborn heart is a will that has locked itself against God's voice.

verses.text_hebrew: ל֗וּ עַ֭מִּי שֹׁמֵ֣עַ לִ֑י יִ֝שְׂרָאֵ֗ל בִּדְרָכַ֥י יְהַלֵּֽכוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Oh that my people had hearkened unto me, and Israel had walked in my ways!
verses.rendering:
If only My people would listen to Me,
if Israel would walk in My ways!
verses.expanded_rendering: The particle lu ('if only, would that') is the Hebrew expression of unfulfilled longing — a wish contrary to fact. God is not issuing a command here; God is expressing desire. Lu ammi shomea li ('if only My people would listen to Me') reveals something about the divine character that systematic theology often obscures: God yearns. The God who spoke the universe into being uses the language of unfulfilled wish when speaking about His people's attention. The parallel — Yisra'el bi-drakhay yehalekhu ('Israel would walk in My ways') — shifts from hearing to walking, from attention to action. God wants both: ears that listen and feet that follow.
translator_notes: The particle lu introduces a counterfactual wish — something desired but not actualized. This is one of the most emotionally revealing statements God makes in the Psalter. It places divine omnipotence alongside divine longing, suggesting that God's power does not override human freedom even when that freedom causes God grief.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֭מְעַט אוֹיְבֵיהֶ֣ם אַכְנִ֑יעַ וְעַ֥ל צָ֝רֵיהֶ֗ם אָשִׁ֥יב יָדִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I should soon have subdued their enemies, and turned my hand against their adversaries.
verses.rendering:
How quickly I would subdue their enemies
and turn My hand against their foes!
translator_notes: The word kim'at ('how little, how quickly, almost') emphasizes the ease and speed with which God would act — the obstacles Israel faces are trivial from God's perspective. The verb akhnia ('I would subdue, I would bring low') is the Hiphil of kana, meaning to force into submission. The phrase ashiv yadi ('I would turn My hand') means to redirect divine power against Israel's oppressors. The conditional remains in force: all of this would happen if Israel would listen.

verses.text_hebrew: מְשַׂנְאֵ֣י יְ֭הוָה יְכַחֲשׁוּ־ל֑וֹ וִיהִ֖י עִתָּ֣ם לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The haters of the LORD should have submitted themselves unto him: but their time should have endured for ever.
verses.rendering:
Those who hate the LORD would cringe before Him,
and their doom would last forever.
translator_notes: The verb yekhachashu ('would cringe, would feign submission') from kachash means to cower, to submit falsely, to cringe in fear. The enemies would not genuinely repent — they would simply be crushed into submission. The phrase ittam le-olam ('their time forever') is ambiguous: it may mean Israel's time of prosperity would last forever, or that the enemies' time of punishment would be permanent. Most interpreters take it as Israel's blessing enduring.

verses.text_hebrew: וַ֭יַּאֲכִילֵהוּ מֵחֵ֣לֶב חִטָּ֑ה וּ֝מִצּ֗וּר דְּבַ֣שׁ אַשְׂבִּיעֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: He should have fed them also with the finest of the wheat: and with honey out of the rock should I have satisfied thee.
verses.rendering:
He would feed him with the finest wheat,
and from the rock I would satisfy you with honey.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes with an image of lavish provision. The phrase chelev chittah ('fat of wheat') means the richest, choicest grain — chelev ('fat') is the word for the best portion, the part reserved for God in sacrifice. God would give Israel the portion He normally receives. And u-mi-tsur devash asbi'ekha ('and from the rock honey I would satisfy you') recalls the wilderness miracles: water from the rock at Meribah (Exodus 17) and Massah, now transformed into honey — the sweetest substance the ancient world knew. The rock that gave water in judgment now offers honey in blessing. The shift from third person ('he would feed him') to second person ('I would satisfy you') in the final line is jarring and intentional — God breaks through the hypothetical to address Israel directly, personally, intimately. The psalm ends not with a completed action but with an unfulfilled offer, hanging open.
translator_notes: The shift from third to second person (from 'him' to 'you') within a single verse is characteristic of prophetic speech, where God moves freely between describing Israel and addressing Israel. The honey from the rock echoes Deuteronomy 32:13. Wild bees nested in rock crevices in ancient Palestine, making 'honey from the rock' both a literal reality and a metaphor for provision from impossible sources.

key_terms.semantic_range: fat, best portion, choicest part; wheat, grain
key_terms.note: chelev literally means 'fat' and is the word for the fatty portions of sacrificial animals reserved for God (Leviticus 3:16: 'all the fat belongs to the LORD'). When applied to wheat, it means the richest, plumpest grain — the best of the harvest. God offers Israel what is normally God's own portion.
